title: Variables & Mutability
description: Understand how Rust binds values to names with let, enforces immutability by default, and how let mut opens a slot for reassignment.

concept.definition: In Rust, variables are immutable by default — once bound, a value cannot change unless you explicitly mark the binding with `mut`. This design catches accidental mutation at compile time.
concept.analogy: An immutable variable is like a sealed envelope: you can read it, but you cannot change its contents without opening a new envelope.
keyPoints: let x = 5; creates an immutable binding
let mut x = 5; allows reassignment
const MAX: u32 = 100; is a compile-time constant, not a variable
Shadowing (re-declaring let x) creates a new binding — the old one is gone

title: Data Types
description: Explore Rust's scalar types (integers, floats, booleans, chars) and compound types (tuples and fixed-size arrays).

concept.definition: Rust is statically typed — every value has a type known at compile time. Scalar types hold a single value; compound types group multiple values into one.
concept.analogy: Think of scalar types as single digits on a scoreboard, and compound types as a row of digits together.
keyPoints: Integer types: i8/u8 through i128/u128, plus isize/usize
Float types: f32 and f64 (default is f64)
bool: true or false
char: a Unicode scalar value, e.g. 'A'
Tuple: fixed-length, mixed types — (1, 2.0, 'a')
Array: fixed-length, same type — [1, 2, 3]

title: Functions
description: Learn how to define functions with fn, pass parameters, and return values using Rust's expression-based syntax.

concept.definition: Functions are named, reusable blocks of code. In Rust, functions use `fn` and the last expression in the body is implicitly returned (no semicolon). Explicit `return` is available but rarely needed.
concept.analogy: A function is a recipe: you give it ingredients (parameters) and it produces a dish (return value).
keyPoints: fn name(param: Type) -> ReturnType { body }
The final expression (no semicolon) is the return value
Statements end with ; and do not produce values
Expressions produce values — e.g. { let x = 5; x + 1 }

title: Control Flow
description: Branch your program with if/else and match. Discover how Rust's if is an expression that can produce a value.

concept.definition: Rust's if/else evaluates a bool condition and runs one branch. Because if is an expression, it can be used on the right-hand side of a let. The match expression exhaustively covers all cases.
concept.analogy: if/else is like a fork in a road; match is like a roundabout with a dedicated exit for every possible direction.
keyPoints: Condition must be bool — no implicit conversion
if/else is an expression: let x = if cond { 1 } else { 2 };
match must cover all variants — the compiler enforces this
_ is the wildcard arm in match

title: Loops
description: Master Rust's three loop forms — loop, while, and for — plus break with a return value and the range syntax.

concept.definition: Rust has three loop constructs: `loop` (infinite, break to exit), `while` (condition-checked on each iteration), and `for` (iterates over a range or collection). `break` can carry a value out of a `loop`.
concept.analogy: loop is a revolving door that only stops when you push the exit bar; while is a turnstile that checks your ticket each time; for is a conveyor belt that stops automatically.
keyPoints: loop { } runs forever until break
break value; returns a value from loop
while condition { }
for x in 0..5 iterates 0,1,2,3,4 (exclusive end)
for x in 0..=5 iterates 0,1,2,3,4,5 (inclusive end)

title: Ownership
description: Rust's ownership system guarantees memory safety without a garbage collector. Learn the three rules and how move semantics work.

concept.definition: Every value in Rust has an owner. When the owner goes out of scope, the value is dropped. Assignment of heap-allocated types (like String) moves ownership — the old binding becomes invalid.
concept.analogy: Ownership is like a physical book — only one person can hold it at a time. Handing it to someone else (move) means you no longer have it.
keyPoints: Rule 1: Each value has exactly one owner
Rule 2: When the owner goes out of scope, the value is dropped
Rule 3: There can only be one owner at a time
Scalar types (i32, bool, etc.) are Copy — assignment duplicates them
String is not Copy — assignment moves the ownership

title: References & Borrowing
description: Borrow a value by reference (&T) or mutable reference (&mut T) to use it without taking ownership.

concept.definition: A reference lets you refer to a value without owning it. Immutable borrows (&T) allow multiple concurrent readers. Mutable borrows (&mut T) allow exactly one writer at a time — no other borrows active.
concept.analogy: Immutable borrowing is like lending a book to many readers at once (all read-only). Mutable borrowing is like lending it to one person with a pen — no one else can read until it's returned.
keyPoints: &T — shared (immutable) reference; many allowed at once
&mut T — exclusive (mutable) reference; only one at a time
You cannot have &mut T and any &T at the same time
References must always be valid — no dangling pointers

title: Structs
description: Group related data into named structs and attach behaviour with impl blocks.

concept.definition: A struct groups named fields of different types into a single type. Methods and associated functions are defined in an `impl` block. `&self` gives read access; `&mut self` gives write access.
concept.analogy: A struct is like a form with labelled fields — name, age, score. impl adds the operations you can do with that form.
keyPoints: struct Name { field: Type, ... }
Instantiate: Name { field: value, ... }
Access fields with dot notation: point.x
impl Name { fn method(&self) { } }
Associated function (no self): Name::new()

title: Enums
description: Define a type that can be one of several variants. Enums in Rust can carry data and are combined with match for exhaustive handling.

concept.definition: An enum lists all possible variants of a type. Each variant can optionally carry data. Rust's Option<T> is a built-in enum: Some(value) or None — the safe replacement for null.
concept.analogy: An enum is like a traffic light: it can only ever be Red, Yellow, or Green — and each state might carry extra info (like countdown seconds).
keyPoints: enum Color { Red, Green, Blue }
Variants can hold data: enum Message { Move { x: i32, y: i32 }, Quit }
Option<T> = Some(T) | None — no null in safe Rust
match is idiomatic for handling enum variants

title: Pattern Matching
description: Use match, if let, and while let to destructure enums, tuples, and structs and act on their contents.

concept.definition: Pattern matching tests a value against a series of patterns and executes the matching arm. Patterns can destructure complex types, bind names to parts of values, and include guards (extra conditions).
concept.analogy: Pattern matching is like sorting mail by shape and label: each slot is a pattern, and the letter falls into the first one that fits.
keyPoints: match value { pattern => expr, ... } — exhaustive
if let Some(x) = opt { } — match one variant, ignore rest
while let Some(x) = iter.next() { } — loop until no match
Guard: pattern if condition => expr
.. ignores remaining fields in a struct or tuple pattern

title: Vectors & Collections
description: Use Vec<T> — Rust's growable array — to store, access, and iterate over lists of values.

concept.definition: Vec<T> is a heap-allocated, growable list of values of a single type. Elements can be pushed, popped, indexed, and iterated. Accessing out-of-bounds with [] panics; use .get(i) for safe Option access.
concept.analogy: A Vec is like a whiteboard list: you can add items to the bottom, erase the last one, or read any entry by its number.
keyPoints: Vec::new() or vec![1, 2, 3] macro
v.push(x) appends; v.pop() removes and returns last
v[i] panics on out-of-bounds; v.get(i) returns Option<&T>
for x in &v iterates by reference (no move)
v.len() returns the count

title: Error Handling
description: Handle failures explicitly with Result<T, E> and Option<T>. Use the ? operator for clean propagation.

concept.definition: Rust has no exceptions. Recoverable errors use Result<T, E> (Ok(T) on success, Err(E) on failure). Missing values use Option<T> (Some(T) or None). The ? operator propagates errors to the caller automatically.
concept.analogy: Result is like a delivery box: it either contains your package (Ok) or a note explaining the failure (Err). The ? operator is an automatic forwarding slip.
keyPoints: Result<T, E>: Ok(value) | Err(error)
Option<T>: Some(value) | None
unwrap() panics on Err/None — only use in prototypes
? in a fn returning Result/Option propagates the error
match and if let give explicit control over Ok/Err branches
